era_context: The Eastern Roman Empire under Justin I is in the middle of his reign (518–527); Justinian is rising in influence. The Sassanid Empire under Khosrow I is consolidating power. In China, the Northern Wei fragmentation continues; competing northern dynasties are consolidating. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization continues to flourish. In Europe, post-Roman Germanic kingdoms are establishing themselves.

events.title: Florentius and Probus serve as Consuls of the Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Florentius and Probus serve as consuls in 521 CE. This consulship reflects the political dynamics of the reign of Justin I and the rising influence of Justinian. The consulship is part of the pattern of aristocratic competition and alliance-building in the Byzantine court.
events.certainty_note: Consular records are well-documented in Byzantine sources and inscriptions.

events.title: Vitalian's rebellion continues in the Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Vitalian, a military commander and general in the Eastern Roman Empire, continues his rebellion against the emperor Justin I. Vitalian's rebellion involves military campaigns in the Balkans and threatens Constantinople. The rebellion is motivated by religious disputes (the Monophysite controversy) and political grievances. Vitalian's forces are eventually defeated, and he is executed. The rebellion is a significant event in the religious and political history of the Byzantine Empire during this period.

sources.name: Procopius of Caesarea, History of the Wars

events.certainty_note: Vitalian's rebellion is well-attested in Byzantine sources, but the exact dates are debated. Some sources place the rebellion in 513–520, others extend it to 522 or 523. The chronology of Vitalian's campaigns and death is uncertain by several years.

events.title: Sassanid-Byzantine conflicts in Mesopotamia
events.description: The Sassanid Empire under Khosrow I and the Eastern Roman Empire under Justin I continue their ongoing border conflicts in Mesopotamia. These conflicts involve raids, fortification building, and diplomatic negotiations. The period sees competition for control of trade routes and buffer states. These conflicts will intensify significantly under Justinian's reign.

sources.name: Procopius of Caesarea, History of the Wars

events.certainty_note: Sassanid-Byzantine conflicts in this period are attested in Procopius and numismatic evidence, but specific events in 521 are not always clearly dated. The broader pattern of conflict is confirmed.

events.title: Maya stela dedication and astronomical observations
events.description: During the Classic Maya period, Maya civilization in Mesoamerica is at a peak of cultural and intellectual achievement. Maya astronomers and mathematicians are maintaining sophisticated calendrical systems and recording astronomical observations on stelae and in codices. The year 521 CE (Long Count 9.4.16.4.9 in the Thompson correlation) falls within a period of intensive stela production at Maya centers, reflecting the height of Classic Maya civilization.

sources.name: Maya stelae and inscriptions (archaeological and epigraphic)

events.certainty_note: Maya Long Count dates are well-established through epigraphic evidence. The correlation with the proleptic Gregorian calendar is standard (Thompson correlation), with minor variants of ±2 years in some scholarly debates.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Vitalian's rebellion is debated. Some sources place the rebellion in 513–520, others extend it to 522 or 523. The exact chronology of Vitalian's campaigns and death is uncertain by several years. Some scholars question whether Vitalian's rebellion extended as late as 521 or whether it had been suppressed by that date.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 521 CE is moderate. Byzantine sources are relatively rich for the Eastern Roman Empire, though specific events in 521 are sometimes conflated with adjacent years. The Vitalian rebellion is well-attested but chronologically uncertain. Chinese sources are abundant but compiled much later. Maya sources are contemporary (inscriptions) but sparse. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Central Asia are poorly documented. The record is weighted toward literate, centralized states and elite activities.

graph_edges.note: Vitalian's rebellion continues across multiple years
